author: Sofia Martínez
title: Coding Bootcamp Marketing: Getting Qualified Leads at $50–120 CAC
description: Combine content marketing with paid ads targeting career changers. We break down the funnel bootcamps need to fill seats profitably.

sections.p: Coding bootcamps live or die on enrollment. We've audited 8 bootcamps over the past 18 months, and the ones scaling at a healthy pace share one trait: they stop chasing warm leads and double down on cold intent. A bootcamp in Austin was spending $4,200/month on retargeting ads to site visitors; their CAC was $240. They were profitable but hit a ceiling fast—you can only retarget so many people. They shifted 60% of budget to cold audiences targeting job titles ('software developer,' 'frontend developer,' 'qa engineer') and skill-specific searches ('learn Python coding bootcamp'). Within 60 days, CAC dropped to $87, and they filled all 28 seats in their next cohort. The math changed because they matched intent: someone searching 'best coding bootcamp for career change' is ready to click. Someone who visited your site three weeks ago isn't.

Bootcamp leads aren't all equal. A high school student interested in coding isn't your customer if you target career changers ages 25–45. Create content assets that pre-qualify leads before they talk to sales. We recommend three tiers:

list: Top of funnel (awareness): 'Is Coding Bootcamp Right for You?' guide, '8 Skills Tech Companies Hire For' checklist, 'Bootcamp vs. College Degree' comparison video
Middle of funnel (consideration): 'Full-Stack Developer Bootcamp Curriculum Breakdown,' 'Real Graduate Job Outcomes' case study (with salary data), 'Bootcamp Cost vs. Salary ROI Calculator'
Bottom of funnel (decision): 'Our Curriculum Includes [These Tools],' 'Meet Your Instructors' video interviews, 'Student Project Portfolio Walk-Through,' free 1-on-1 career counseling call

sections.p: One bootcamp we worked with created a 15-minute YouTube video titled '3 Months of Coding: What I Actually Learned.' It featured a real graduate (hired two weeks after graduation at a $72k salary) walking through projects, talking candidly about the difficulty, and discussing job placement support. That single video generated 340 qualified leads over eight weeks. Why? It answered the hardest question career changers ask: 'Will this actually work for someone like me?'

sections.h2: Google Ads: Target Job-Seeker and Skill-Search Keywords

sections.p: Don't bid on generic terms like 'coding bootcamp.' Target intent-rich keywords where career changers already have their hand up. Set up three Google Ads campaigns:

list: Campaign 1 (High intent): 'coding bootcamp [city],' 'best bootcamp for career change,' 'bootcamp vs college degree,' 'learn full stack development fast'—bid $1.20–2.50/click, expect 8–12% CTR
Campaign 2 (Skill-specific): 'learn Python bootcamp,' 'javascript bootcamp near me,' 'react.js course with job placement'—bid $0.80–1.80/click, expect 6–10% CTR
Campaign 3 (Job-title targeting): 'career change to software development,' 'how to become a web developer,' 'entry level coding jobs no experience'—bid $0.90–2.00/click, expect 7–11% CTR

sections.p: One bootcamp in Chicago ran Campaign 1 keywords at a $1.80 avg bid with a landing page specific to Chicago-based students ('Your 12-Week Path to [City] Tech Jobs'). CTR was 9.2%, conversion to lead form was 14%, and CAC was $98. They weren't cheapest, but they were most relevant. Google rewarded that with a 7.2 Quality Score on those keywords, lowering their actual cost-per-click to $1.32.

sections.quote: Bootcamps obsess over brand keywords and get defensive about competitor bidding. Stop. Your competitor's name isn't your customer. 'Learn to code' and job titles are.

sections.h2: Meta/Facebook Ads: Layer Lookalike and Interest Audiences

sections.p: Google is for intent. Meta is for interest stacking and reaching people in the consideration phase. Set up two Meta campaigns running simultaneously:

list: Campaign A: Lookalike audiences based on (a) past applicants, (b) form completers, (c) enrolled students. Use 1% and 2% lookalikes. Bid $2–5 CPC, expect 3–7% CTR, 8–15% conversion to lead form

Campaign B: Interest-stacked audiences: 'Career change,' 'Software development,' 'Online learning,' 'Professional development' + age 25–50 + college-educated + US/Canada. Bid $3–6 CPC, expect 2–5% CTR, 6–12% conversion

sections.p: A bootcamp in Seattle ran a lookalike campaign from 180 past applicants (their best source). The 1% lookalike reached 240,000 people. Over 30 days, they spent $1,840, got 67 leads, and 9 enrollments. CAC: $204. Higher than Google, but that's normal—Meta is for reach and awareness-to-consideration, not final intent. They balanced it by running Google simultaneously at 60% of budget and Meta at 40%.

sections.h2: Free Tools That Prove ROI Before You Enroll

sections.p: Bootcamps with strong conversion rates use free lead magnets that feel genuine, not manipulative. Create one or two of these:

list: Career Path Quiz: 'Which Tech Role Fits You?' (3-min quiz leading to a custom report on roles, salary ranges, and skills needed)
ROI Calculator: 'Calculate Your Bootcamp Payoff' (student inputs current salary, desired role, time to employment—tool shows months-to-break-even)
Free Email Course: '5 Days to Understanding Full-Stack Development' (daily emails explaining frontend, backend, databases, APIs, deployment)
Curriculum Comparison PDF: 'Bootcamp vs. College vs. Self-Teaching' (honest breakdown of cost, time, job outcomes for each)

sections.p: One bootcamp created a 2-minute 'Salary vs. Time Investment' interactive tool. Candidates input their target salary, and the tool showed: bootcamp ($15k, 3 months, $65k avg starting salary) vs. college degree ($100k, 4 years, $75k avg starting salary) vs. self-teaching (free, 12+ months, $50k avg, harder path). It wasn't a trap—it was honest. That tool converted 22% of visitors who interacted with it. Why? Because it addressed the real question: 'Is this worth the sacrifice?'

sections.h2: Close the Loop: Sales + Retention Data

sections.p: Your marketing data is worthless if sales doesn't use it. Set up a CRM (HubSpot free, Pipedrive, or Salesforce) and track: lead source, date of inquiry, date of application, date of acceptance, cohort start date, job placement outcome. After 40+ enrollments, you'll see patterns.

One bootcamp discovered 34% of their leads came from Google, but only 18% converted to enrollment. Meanwhile, referrals were 8% of leads but 38% conversion. They increased referral incentives ($250 per enrollment) and saw referral leads jump to 14% of total, with the same 38% conversion. The data told them to double down on where trust was highest.
